illustrative-note: Maria and her 400 comments are just an example, not a promise. Where the line really falls — how much you can safely hand over — changes with the kind of work, how well you steer the tool, and how well it works with you. Treat it as a feel for the idea.

Maria's a stand-in, not a promise. Where the line really falls depends on the kind of work, how well you steer the tool, and how well it works with you — the example shows the shape of the idea, not your exact answer.
Maria's a stand-in, not a promise. Where the line really falls depends on the work itself, how well you steer the tool — and how well it knows when to back off and hand you the wheel.

Read it as illustrative. Where the line falls swings with the work (rote vs. genuinely novel), how well you drive the tool — and how well it drives you back, including whether it backs off when it is out of its depth.

Illustrative, not literal. Where the line falls is a product of the task's nature, how well you drive the harness, how well the harness drives you, and — the part most tools get wrong — whether it backs off when out of its depth instead of shipping confident error.
hero-title: What is Augmented Intelligence (AI That Helps You)?
lead: Augmented intelligence just means you and the machine work together. The machine is fast. You make the choices — but does that really leave you in charge, or quietly push you aside? That's worth deciding for yourself. And the best part might not be the AI itself; it could be the extra time you get, and what you choose to do with it.

Augmented intelligence pairs your judgment with the machine's speed. Does that keep you in charge the whole time, or not? You can test it for yourself. The point isn't really the AI — it's the time it hands back to you, and what you do with that time.

Augmented intelligence pairs your judgment with the machine's speed. Does that keep you in charge, or quietly sideline you? Try it below. The point isn't the AI — it's what you do with the time it gives back.
Augmented intelligence pairs your judgment with the machine's speed. In charge, or sidelined? You decide. The point isn't the AI; it's what you do with the reclaimed time.
Augmented intelligence: your judgment plus machine speed. In charge, or sidelined? You decide. The point is the time reclaimed, not the AI.
enduring-idea: One to test below: AI is a tool, and you are the one who tells it what to do — so what would you give it, and what would you keep? Try handing it the boring, bulky work and holding onto the thinking, then see for yourself which choices you'd actually want to keep.

A question to carry in: if AI is a tool you direct, what should you hand off and what should you keep? Slide the work below — give it the heavy volume, hold onto the judgment — and decide where every decision that matters belongs.
A question to carry in: if AI is a tool you direct, what should you hand off and what should you keep? Hand it the volume, keep the judgment — and decide where every decision that matters belongs.
Worth testing: AI is a tool you direct. Hand off the volume, keep the judgment — but where does each decision that matters belong? You decide below.
Test it: AI is a tool you direct. Offload volume, keep judgment — but which decisions are yours? You decide.
example-intro: Here's one real job to test it on. Meet Maria. She runs a busy clinic, and she has to give the board a report on 400 patient comments by Friday. That's a lot. Below, you can try each way of getting the job done and see what happens.
Here it is in one real job. Maria runs a busy clinic, and she owes the board a report on 400 patient comments by Friday. Try each different way of getting it done:

One job: Maria owes the board a report on 400 patient comments by Friday. Try each approach:
explorable-takeaway: Did any single setting fill up both bars? Have a look. The one that does is where the machine handles the big pile of work and you keep the thinking — like what matters most, the mistakes only a person would spot, and the final say. The middle button is "AI that replaces you": it's fast, but nobody is checking it, so its confident mistakes slip right through. If you'd draw the line somewhere else, slide it again and see.

Did any setting fill both bars? The one that does is where the machine handles the volume and you keep the judgment (the priorities, the mistakes only a person would catch, the final call). "AI that replaces you" is the middle button: fast, but with no one watching, its confident errors sail straight through. Slide it again if you'd put the line elsewhere.

Did any setting fill both bars? The one that does is where the machine handles the volume and you keep the judgment (the priorities, the mistakes only a person would catch, the final call). "AI that replaces you" is the middle button: fast, but with no one in the loop, its confident errors sail straight through. Slide it again if you'd draw the line elsewhere.

Did any setting fill both bars? The one that does: the machine handles volume, you keep judgment (priorities, the mistakes only a person catches, the final call). The middle button — "AI that replaces you" — is fast, but with no one in the loop, its confident errors sail through. Disagree? Slide it again. This is what Engelbart meant in 1962 by augmenting human intellect — raising “the capability of a man to approach a complex problem situation… and to derive solutions to problems”, with the human keeping the goal (Engelbart, 1962).

Which settings fill both bars? Where machine handles volume, you keep judgment (priorities, human-only catches, final call). The middle button — "AI replaces you" — is fast but unsupervised, so confident errors pass through. Judge for yourself. The frame is Engelbart's, 1962 — augmenting human intellect: “increasing the capability of a man to approach a complex problem situation… and to derive solutions to problems.” The machine extends the human; it never supplies the judgment (Engelbart, 1962).
recall-question: When you give a job to AI, which part do you keep, and which part do you let go of?
When you hand work to AI, what should you keep, and what should you give away?
When you hand work to AI, what do you keep and what do you give away?
Handing work to AI: what do you keep, and what do you give away?
AI handoff: keep what, give away what?
recall-answer: Give away the volume — that's the repeated, mechanical work a machine does well. Keep the judgment — that's the context, what matters most, the right-and-wrong calls, and the final say. Those stay with you.
Give away the volume — the repetitive, mechanical work. Keep the judgment — the context, the priorities, the ethics, and the final call.
Give away the volume — the repetitive, mechanical work. Keep the judgment — context, priorities, ethics, and the final call.
Give away the volume (repetitive, mechanical work). Keep the judgment: context, priorities, ethics, the final call.
Give away volume (repetitive, mechanical work). Keep judgment: context, priorities, ethics, final call.

tryit-intro: This idea only really clicks when you try it on something real from your own life. So pick one task you have to do this week:

tryit-step-1: Write down the task in just one short line.
Write the task down in one line.
Write the task in one line.
State the task in one line.
Name the task in one line.
tryit-step-2: Now split that task into two piles. One pile is the volume — the repeated, mechanical work that a machine could draft, sort, or search for you. The other pile is the judgment — the parts that need your own context, your sense of right and wrong, your taste, or your final say.
Split it into two parts. Volume is the repetitive, mechanical work that could be drafted, sorted, or searched. Judgment is the part that needs your context, ethics, taste, or final call.
Split it into volume (repetitive, mechanical — could be drafted, sorted, or searched) and judgment (needs your context, ethics, taste, or final call).
Split it into volume (repetitive, mechanical — draftable, sortable, searchable) and judgment (needs your context, ethics, taste, or final call).
Split into volume (mechanical: draft/sort/search) and judgment (your context, ethics, taste, final call).
tryit-step-3: Now decide which of the volume parts you would hand over to AI. Then mark the exact moment where you would step back in to check the work, just like Maria did with her report.
Decide which volume parts you would hand to AI — and pick the exact point where you'd step back in to verify, the way Maria did.
Decide which volume parts you'd hand to AI — and the exact point where you'd stay in the loop to verify, the way Maria did.
Decide which volume parts to hand to AI — and the exact point where you'd stay in the loop to verify, as Maria did.
Decide which volume to delegate to AI, and the exact checkpoint where you verify — as Maria did.
tryit-closer: That split is the move to practise — there's no secret extra step waiting. Try it five times this week and see whether it stops feeling like an exercise and starts to feel like something you reach for without thinking.
